sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A psychology lab tested whether framing affected performance on a difficult quantitative reasoning test. Participants were randomly assigned to a diagnostic condition, told the test measured innate quantitative ability; a challenge condition, told the test was a set of learnable puzzles; or a values-affirmation condition completed before the diagnostic framing. Mean scores were 61%, 69%, and 72%. Self-reported worry about confirming stereotypes was highest in the diagnostic condition and lowest in the values-affirmation condition.
questions.stem: Which condition produced the highest mean score?

choices.C: All conditions produced the same score

explanations.D: Correct. The values-affirmation condition produced the highest mean score at 72%, compared with 69% in the challenge condition and 61% in the diagnostic condition. The performance pattern is consistent with reduced threat-related worry.
explanations.A: The diagnostic condition produced the lowest score, not the highest.
explanations.B: The challenge condition improved performance but did not exceed values affirmation.
explanations.C: The scores differ by meaningful amounts across conditions.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean quantitative reasoning scores across three framing conditions.

questions.stem: The diagnostic framing most likely increased which process?

explanations.A: Correct. Telling participants the test measured innate ability can make negative stereotypes feel relevant and raise worry about confirming them. That worry is the core of stereotype threat.
explanations.B: Social loafing concerns reduced effort in group tasks, not identity-related performance pressure.
explanations.C: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced response to repeated sensory input.
explanations.D: Object permanence is a developmental concept unrelated to test framing.

questions.stem: Why might values affirmation improve performance?

choices.C: It can protect self-integrity and reduce threat-related cognitive load
choices.A: It teaches all quantitative content in a few minutes
choices.B: It makes the test easier by changing the questions

explanations.C: Correct. Values affirmation can reduce the psychological pressure of an identity threat by reminding people of broader sources of self-worth. That may free attention and working memory for the task.
explanations.A: The passage does not say the affirmation taught math content.
explanations.B: The same kind of test was used; the manipulation targeted framing and self-threat.
explanations.D: No intervention eliminates all individual differences.

questions.stem: A school applying these findings should most likely do what?

choices.B: Frame difficult work as learnable and reduce identity-threatening cues
choices.A: Emphasize that every test reveals fixed innate ability
choices.C: Tell students stereotype worry is a personal weakness
choices.D: Remove all challenging work from the curriculum

explanations.B: Correct. The challenge and affirmation conditions performed better than diagnostic framing. A practical application is to preserve rigor while reducing cues that imply fixed ability or stereotype confirmation.
explanations.A: Fixed-ability framing resembles the lowest-performing condition.
explanations.C: Blaming students for worry would likely intensify threat rather than reduce it.
explanations.D: The study supports changing framing, not eliminating challenge.

questions.stem: Which design feature strengthens causal inference in the study?

choices.A: Participants were randomly assigned to framing conditions
choices.B: The study measured only one score

explanations.A: Correct. Random assignment helps make the framing condition the major systematic difference between groups. That strengthens the claim that framing and affirmation contributed to performance differences.
explanations.B: Measuring one score does not itself strengthen causal inference.
explanations.C: A low score is an outcome, not a design feature.
explanations.D: Difficulty may be necessary to evoke threat, but it does not replace random assignment.

questions.passage: A hospital required staff to complete an implicit-bias workshop. The workshop explained automatic associations, perspective-taking, and structured decision aids for pain assessment. Six months later, chart audits found that documentation of pain reassessment improved in all units, but interviews suggested that the largest changes occurred where supervisors also changed workflow checklists. Researchers warned that one-time workshops may raise awareness, but durable behavior change often requires institutional supports.
questions.stem: The workshop topic most directly concerns which construct?

explanations.D: Correct. The passage refers to automatic associations that can shape judgment outside conscious intention. That is the central idea of implicit bias.
explanations.A: Crystallized intelligence involves accumulated knowledge, not automatic social associations.
explanations.B: Habituation is decreased response to repeated stimuli.
explanations.C: Role exit is leaving a social role and does not explain pain-assessment bias.

choices.C: They helped translate awareness into consistent clinical behavior
choices.A: They made bias impossible in every future decision
choices.D: They prevented staff from reassessing pain

explanations.C: Correct. The passage says the largest changes occurred where supervisors changed workflow checklists. That suggests institutional supports helped convert workshop ideas into routine behavior.
explanations.A: No checklist can guarantee the complete elimination of bias.
explanations.B: Structured aids support judgment; they do not replace all clinical thinking.
explanations.D: The audits showed improved reassessment documentation, not prevention of reassessment.

questions.stem: What is the limitation of relying only on one-time workshops?

choices.B: Awareness may fade or fail to change behavior without structural reinforcement
choices.C: Bias is always conscious and deliberate
choices.D: Institutional supports are irrelevant to behavior

explanations.B: Correct. The researchers warn that durable change often requires supports beyond awareness. People may understand bias but still act within routines that make biased or inconsistent decisions more likely.
explanations.A: Workshops can teach concepts; the question is whether concepts alone change behavior durably.
explanations.C: Implicit bias is often automatic rather than fully conscious.
explanations.D: The passage specifically says institutional supports mattered.

questions.stem: Which intervention would best match the researchers' recommendation?

choices.D: Pair bias education with structured pain-assessment checklists and supervisor follow-up
choices.A: Offer a single lecture and make no workflow changes
choices.C: Tell staff that good intentions alone prevent bias

explanations.D: Correct. The strongest changes were linked to both education and workflow changes. Combining training with structured decision aids and follow-up addresses individual awareness and institutional practice.
explanations.A: A lecture alone is the limited approach the researchers caution against.
explanations.B: Removing reassessment would undermine the care process being improved.
explanations.C: Good intentions do not reliably prevent automatic associations from influencing behavior.

questions.stem: Which outcome would best show durable behavior change?

choices.A: Sustained improvement in pain reassessment documentation and patient reports across multiple audits
choices.B: Immediate satisfaction with the workshop only
choices.C: A single staff member describing the workshop as interesting
choices.D: A change in hospital logo design

explanations.A: Correct. Durability requires repeated evidence that clinical behavior changed over time, ideally using chart audits and patient-centered outcomes. Immediate reactions are less informative than sustained practice change.
explanations.B: Workshop satisfaction measures acceptability, not durable behavior.
explanations.C: One anecdote does not establish unit-wide or sustained change.
explanations.D: Logo design is unrelated to pain assessment or bias reduction.
